title: ArrayQueue: Build It With Paper
subtitle: See how a circular array queue works by physically moving the front pointer and tracking size, then watch it resize.
encouragement: Queues are everywhere: print jobs, web server requests, breadth-first search. Once you see the circular trick and the resize with your own hands, implementing one in code becomes straightforward.
realWorldExample: Think of a line at a coffee shop. New customers join at the back. The person at the front gets served next. That's a queue: First In, First Out. The circular part? Imagine the line wraps around a round counter so the back of the line can reuse space freed by people who already left.

materials.detail: First strip: draw 4 equally-spaced boxes. Second strip: draw 8 boxes. The second strip is for the resize step later.
To draw boxes, write index numbers, and label pointers.
Write these letters on them: A, B, C, D, E, F
This marks "front" (where we dequeue from). We track the size with a number on paper, not a second pointer.

setup: Draw 4 boxes on your first paper strip. Number them 0 through 3 underneath.
Place the FRONT paperclip at index 0.
Write "size = 0" on a corner of your paper. This is the only other thing we track.
The queue doesn't store a "back" pointer. Instead, the back position is always computed: back = (front + size) % capacity. We'll calculate it each time.
Keep the second strip (8 boxes) aside for now. You'll need it when the queue runs out of space.

steps.instruction: Let's enqueue the letter A. Where does it go? Compute the back position: (front + size) % capacity = (0 + 0) % 4 = 0.
steps.action: Place A into box 0. Update your size: cross out 0 and write size = 1.
steps.conceptNote: Enqueue computes where the back is using (front + size) % capacity, places the item there, then increments size. No "back" pointer is stored; it's always derived.
steps.hint: The back position is just a calculation, not a stored value. This saves us from having to maintain two pointers.

steps.instruction:
Enqueue B, then C.

For B: back = (0 + 1) % 4 = 1. Place B at index 1. size = 2.
For C: back = (0 + 2) % 4 = 2. Place C at index 2. size = 3.
steps.action: Place B at index 1, C at index 2. Update size to 3.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [A][B][C][ ]. front: 0, size: 3. One empty slot left.

steps.instruction: Now let's dequeue. Which item gets removed? Look at where FRONT is pointing.
steps.action: Remove the A card from index 0. Advance FRONT: front = (0 + 1) % 4 = 1. Decrement size: size = 2.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [ ][B][C][ ]. front: 1, size: 2. A is gone.
steps.conceptNote: FIFO: First In, First Out. A was enqueued first, so A is dequeued first. Dequeue advances front with the modulo formula and decrements size.
steps.hint: Notice index 0 is now empty. That space will be reused when the queue wraps around; that's the circular trick.

steps.instruction: Enqueue D. Compute the back: (front + size) % capacity = (1 + 2) % 4 = 3. Place D at index 3. size = 3.
steps.action: Place D at index 3. Update size to 3.

steps.instruction: Enqueue E. Compute the back: (1 + 3) % 4 = 0. The back wraps around to index 0!
steps.action: Place E at index 0. Update size to 4. The array is now FULL (size == capacity).
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [E][B][C][D]. front: 1, size: 4. Every slot is occupied.
steps.conceptNote: The modulo is what makes it circular. (1 + 3) % 4 = 0 wraps back to the beginning. The slot freed when A was dequeued is now reused by E.
steps.hint: Imagine bending your paper strip into a ring so index 3 connects to index 0. That's the mental model.

steps.instruction:
Now we want to enqueue F. But size == capacity (4 == 4). The queue is full. There's nowhere to put F!

We need to resize before we can enqueue F.
steps.conceptNote: The enqueue method checks if size == capacity: resize() BEFORE placing the new item. So the resize happens first, then F gets placed in the bigger array.
steps.hint: This is the same idea as ArrayList: when you run out of room, double the capacity, copy everything over, then continue.

steps.instruction: RESIZE: Double the capacity from 4 to 8. Grab your second paper strip (the one with 8 boxes). Number them 0 through 7.
steps.action: Place the new strip below the old one. Now we copy elements in queue order (starting from front), NOT in array order. Queue order is: B, C, D, E.
steps.checkpoint: You have two strips: the old one (4 boxes, full) and the new one (8 boxes, empty).
steps.conceptNote: We don't copy array positions blindly. We copy starting from FRONT, going in queue order. This "straightens out" the circular wrap so the data starts at index 0 in the new array.
steps.hint: The old array has E at index 0, B at 1, C at 2, D at 3. But queue order is B, C, D, E (starting from front = 1, wrapping around).

steps.instruction: Copy elements in queue order. Start from the old front (index 1) and walk forward, wrapping around.
steps.action:
From the old strip, pick up cards in queue order and place them in the new strip starting at index 0:

1. Old index 1 (B) → New index 0
2. Old index 2 (C) → New index 1
3. Old index 3 (D) → New index 2
4. Old index 0 (E) → New index 3

Set front = 0. Size is still 4. Capacity is now 8.
steps.conceptNote:
In code, the copy loop uses modulo:
for i in range(size):
  new_arr[i] = old_arr[(front + i) % old_cap]
front = 0

Size stays the same. Only front and capacity change. The resize is O(n) but happens rarely, so the amortized cost of enqueue is still O(1).
steps.hint: Push the old strip aside. You won't need it anymore. The new strip is your queue now.

steps.instruction: Now we have room! Enqueue F (the item that triggered the resize). Compute back: (0 + 4) % 8 = 4.
steps.action: Place F at index 4. Update size to 5.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [B][C][D][E][F][ ][ ][ ]. front: 0, size: 5. Plenty of room.
steps.conceptNote: After the resize, everything works the same. The circular behavior, enqueue, dequeue, the modulo formula are all identical. The only difference is more capacity.

steps.instruction: Let's dequeue a few to see the new array in action. Dequeue twice.
steps.action: Dequeue B (front advances to 1, size = 4). Dequeue C (front advances to 2, size = 3).

steps.instruction: Enqueue a few more to see the wrap-around happen in the larger array. Write G, H, I, J on scraps of paper.
steps.action: Enqueue G: back = (2+3)%8 = 5. Enqueue H: back = (2+4)%8 = 6. Enqueue I: back = (2+5)%8 = 7. Enqueue J: back = (2+6)%8 = 0. Wrap! Size = 7.
steps.conceptNote: The wrap-around works the same in the bigger array: (2 + 6) % 8 = 0. Same modulo formula, bigger capacity. Notice we never need to store "back" anywhere; it's always just (front + size) % capacity.

steps.instruction:
Let's summarize what you just saw:

• Enqueue: compute (front + size) % capacity to find where to place the item. Increment size. O(1).
• Dequeue: remove from front, advance front = (front + 1) % capacity. Decrement size. O(1).
• Wrap-around: the % capacity makes the array circular.
• Resize: when size == capacity and we need to enqueue, double the capacity and copy in queue order. O(n) but rare, so amortized O(1).
• No "back" pointer: the class only stores front and size. Back is always derived.
steps.action: Look at both paper strips side by side. The old one (4 boxes) was full. The new one (8 boxes) has the same data but more room. The elements were copied in queue order, not array order.
steps.conceptNote: This is the same resize strategy used by ArrayList: double the capacity when full. The amortized analysis is similar. On average, each enqueue does O(1) work even though occasional resizes cost O(n).

reflection.question: How is a queue different from a stack?
reflection.answer: A stack is LIFO (Last In, First Out), a queue is FIFO (First In, First Out). In a stack, you add and remove from the same end. In a queue, you add at the back and remove from the front.

reflection.question: Why use a circular array instead of shifting all elements forward on each dequeue?
reflection.answer: Shifting every element costs O(n) per dequeue. With a circular array, dequeue just moves the front pointer: O(1). No data moves at all.

reflection.question: During resize, why do we copy elements in queue order (front to back) instead of array order (0 to capacity-1)?
reflection.answer: Because the data may wrap around. In the example, the queue order was B, C, D, E but they were stored as E(0), B(1), C(2), D(3). Copying in array order would scramble the queue. Copying in queue order straightens it out so front resets to 0.

reflection.question: Why doesn't ArrayQueue store a "back" pointer?
reflection.answer: Because back can always be computed: (front + size) % capacity. Storing it would be redundant. Keeping only front and size is cleaner and avoids the ambiguity of front == back meaning either empty or full.

reflection.question: What's the wrap-around formula for the enqueue position?
reflection.answer: back = (front + size) % capacity. The modulo operator wraps the index back to 0 when it reaches the end of the array.
